original_abstract: This newly revised and updated second edition of *International Organization: Theories and Institutions* provides a comprehensive introduction to the study of international organizations within the field of International Relations. Designed as a concise bridge between introductory textbooks and complex primary sources, the book offers upper-level undergraduate and graduate students the analytical tools necessary to understand how international organizations work, what they are designed to achieve, and how they shape global politics. Rather than offering a purely descriptive history of specific agencies, this edition focuses on bridging the gap between theory and practice, applying diverse theoretical frameworks—from realism and liberalism to institutionalism and constructivism—to real-world issues such as global security, human rights, international finance, and environmental governance.
humanized_abstract: The second edition of this text has been heavily revised and updated and is designed to provide an overarching perspective on the study of international organizations within the International Relations discipline. Rather than being a brief historical look at one or two specific agencies, this work attempts to bridge the gap between introductory texts and advanced reading of the primary materials, and is designed to complement existing works on this topic. Written for the advanced upper-level undergraduate and graduate student, this book on International Organization: Theories and Institutions aims to allow students, after the most general background reading provided in introductory texts, to look in some depth into those aspects of international organizations that will enable them to determine the role of the latter in the international political process, assess the effectiveness of these organizations in contributing to international peace and security and to the betterment of the conditions of the international community, and form independent judgments as to their relative importance in the world of international politics. While not based on a deterministic theory of international politics or the functionalist view of institutional development, the second edition of this work primarily addresses itself to the theory-based analysis of the role and impact of international institutions on major contemporary issues identified for the international community — international peace and security, respect for human rights, the smooth functioning of international commerce, and the promotion of environmental protection. The breadth of international issues discussed allows for the application of many of the competing perspectives, from the neorealism and neo-liberalism to institutionalism and constructivism, to selected themes in international relations.
